title: 顺水推舟
summary:
While discussing the nature of sin and luxury with Lynn Marchmont at Frognack, Hercule Poirot is interrupted by a frightened maid who reports that the lady upstairs, Rosalind, cannot be woken and feels cold. Poirot rushes to Rosalind's bedroom and finds her lying on a four-poster bed. After checking her pulse, Poirot informs Lynn that Rosalind has been dead for some time and died in her sleep.

Poirot discovers a small white box by the bedside containing three remaining pills labeled "Take one before bedtime." On a desk nearby, he finds a piece of paper with untidy handwriting in which Rosalind expresses feelings of wickedness and a need to find peace, ending the note abruptly with a dash. David Hunter, Rosalind's brother, rushes into the room and accuses Lynn and others of murdering Rosalind for her money. When Poirot suggests Rosalind may not have been murdered, David points to her healthy heart and nervous problems as reasons to doubt a natural death.

Inspector Spence arrives and proposes an alternative theory: that Rosalind may have killed a man named Yadon in a fit of madness while in the Wormsley Valley, rather than being in London as David claimed. In a subsequent discussion at the police station, Spence admits he had focused on David's alibi while overlooking Rosalind's. He suggests Rosalind might have attacked Yadon in a rage and notes her recent dazed behavior. Poirot clarifies that the pills found by Rosalind's bed were merely harmless bromide used for calming nerves.
